text: Last class I replied politely -- Certainly for yes, Unfortunately, I cannot for no.
Now I must ask a big favour and refuse another request -- and stay polite.
Request
Favour
Wonder
Soften
Refuse
Decline
Afraid
Appreciate
I was wondering if you could bring the date forward.
Would it be possible to send the prices by Friday?
I am afraid we cannot increase the order, as our budget is fixed.
We must respectfully decline, but we could review it next quarter.
Percival, before I send this, I was wondering if you could check the tone. I have to ask Mr. Costa a big favour.
Of course. What is the request?
I wrote: Would it be possible to bring the delivery date forward by a week? But he also asked us to increase the order.
We cannot do that. Did you refuse politely?
Yes. I wrote: I am afraid we cannot increase the order, as our budget is fixed. Then I apologised and offered to review it next quarter.
Perfect. The request is gentle and the no is soft, with a reason. I appreciate the clear tone. You can send it.
I was wondering if you could help me.
Would it be possible to send it by Friday?
I am afraid we cannot, as our budget is fixed.
We must respectfully decline for now.
I really appreciate your understanding.

Hi Percival, this is Karen. I am reading you a difficult email before I send it, so you can tell me if it sounds polite enough. It is to the supplier, Mr. Costa, and I have to ask for two big things and refuse one of his requests. Here it is. Dear Mr. Costa, I hope you are well. I am writing to ask a small favour. I was wondering if you could possibly bring the delivery date forward by one week. I know this is short notice, so please let me know if that is a problem. There is one more thing. Would it be possible to send the updated prices by Friday? Regarding your own request, I am afraid we cannot increase the order this month, as our budget is fixed. I am sorry about that, and I do appreciate your patience. If it helps, we could review it again next quarter. Thank you very much for your understanding. Kind regards, Karen. That is the whole email. Do you think the no sounds soft enough, or is it too direct?

Hello, this is Daniel from the supplier's office, leaving a short voicemail in reply to Karen's email. Thank you for your message and for asking so politely. About the delivery date: I am afraid we cannot bring it forward by a full week, but we could possibly manage three days earlier. I hope that helps. Regarding the updated prices, certainly, I will send them by Friday, as you requested. And there is no need to apologise about the order. We completely understand that the budget is fixed, and we are happy to wait and review it next quarter. I really appreciate how clearly you explained everything. Please do not hesitate to ask if you need any other favour. Thank you again, and have a good week.

First, Karen greets the supplier warmly and says she hopes he is well. Then, she makes her first request gently, asking if he could possibly bring the delivery date forward. After that, she adds a second polite request about the updated prices. Later, she refuses his request, saying she is afraid they cannot increase the order, and softens it with a reason and an apology. Finally, she offers an alternative for next quarter and thanks him for his understanding.
